segments.paradiso-26-g34: Sternel is an archaic/poetic verb form of sternere placed before its object for emphasis; it functions here as a present-tense, third-person action 'spreads/strew[s] forth'. The phrase that follows, 'la voce del verace autore', is the direct object (noun phrase) with the prepositional genitive 'del verace autore' specifying whose voice it is.
segments.paradiso-26-g35: 'che' is a relative pronoun referring back to 'autore' (who); 'dice' is present indicative, 3rd person singular, 'says'; 'a Moïsè' marks the indirect object (to Moses) with the preposition a + proper name.
segments.paradiso-26-g36: 'di sé parlando' is a participial/gerund phrase that qualifies the speech: 'speaking of himself'. 'di' introduces the reflexive pronoun 'sé' (himself), and 'parlando' is the present participle/gerund of parlare ('speaking'), indicating simultaneous action to 'dice'.
segments.paradiso-26-g37: Direct quotation introduced by the participial phrase. 'Io' is the explicit subject pronoun; 'ti' is the clitic object (to you); 'farò' is future indicative, 1st person singular of fare used periphrastically with the infinitive 'vedere' to mean 'I will make (you) see' or 'I will show you'; 'ogne valore' is the object of 'farò vedere' meaning 'every (kind of) power/valor'.
segments.paradiso-26-g38: Sternilmi is a contracted/poetic form combining the verb (stern- 'strew/spread' or 'overthrow') with the enclitic pronoun 'mi' (to me), so the verb precedes the subject 'tu' for emphasis: 'you spread it/to me again'. 'ancora' adds the sense 'again/still'.
segments.paradiso-26-g39: 'incominciando' is a present participle/gerund 'beginning', introducing a subordinate phrase that describes what the preceding action does; 'l’alto preconio' is the direct object (the lofty proclamation/praise). The relative clause 'che grida l’arcano' has 'che' = 'which', 'grida' = present indicative 'cries/shouts', and 'l’arcano' = 'the secret/mystery' as the subject; the series 'di qui là giù' gives locations 'from here, thence, below', and 'sovra ogne altro bando' means 'over/above every other bando' (bando functions as a substantive here), completing the description of the proclamation.
segments.paradiso-26-g40: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; 'io' is the subject pronoun used for emphasis; 'udi’' is the contracted past-tense form of udire (historical past), 'I heard', marking Dante's narrative report of speech.
segments.paradiso-26-g41: This is direct speech. 'Per intelletto umano e per autoritadi a lui concorde' is an instrumental/causal phrase: 'by human intellect and by authorities that agree with it' ('concorde' adjectival, 'a lui' = 'with it/him' referring to intelletto). 'd’i tuoi amori' is a prepositional phrase 'of your loves' (i.e., concerning your affections), and the clause ends with the imperative or hortative 'a Dio guarda il sovrano' — 'O sovereign, regard/look to God' ('guarda' as present imperative/indicative addressed to the addressee).
segments.paradiso-26-g42: 'Ma' is the conjunction 'but'; 'dì' is the second-person singular imperative 'say' (addressing the poet/speaker), and 'ancor' = 'still/further'. The conditional clause 'se tu senti altre corde tirarti verso lui' uses 'se' = 'if/whether', 'tu senti' present indicative 'you feel', 'altre corde' (literally 'other strings'—a poetic image for other attractions), and 'tirarti' is the infinitive with enclitic 'ti' meaning 'to pull/draw you' toward 'lui'.
segments.paradiso-26-g43: 'sì che' introduces the result ('so that'); 'tu suone' has 'suone' (present tense of suonare) meaning 'you sound' or 'you give voice' (here intransitive/figurative). 'con quanti denti' is an ablative/instrumental-like phrase 'with how many teeth'; 'questo amor ti morde' is a final clause 'this love bites you' (present tense 'morde' = 'bites/torments'), completing the idea: make known how intensely love pains you.
segments.paradiso-26-g1: Mentr' is an apocopated/poetic form of mentre ('while'). io dubbiava uses the imperfect indicative (dubbiava) to show an ongoing past state: 'I was doubting/hesitating'. per here means 'because of' or 'on account of' and governs the following noun phrase lo viso spento ('the extinguished/dim face'), where lo viso is an older article + noun order and spento is a past participle/adjective meaning 'dim' or 'extinguished'.
segments.paradiso-26-g2: de la fulgida fiamma is a source phrase ('from the resplendent flame'); de is an archaic variant of da ('from'). che lo spense is a relative clause modifying fiamma, with spense as passato remoto (3rd sg) 'extinguished it'. The main clause then inverts subject and verb: uscì un spiro ('a breath came out'), and the following che mi fece attento is another relative clause where fece (passato remoto) is causative 'made me attentive'.
segments.paradiso-26-g3: dicendo is a gerund (present participle) from dire 'to say', used here to attach the reported speech to the action just described: 'a breath came forth, saying...'.
segments.paradiso-26-g4: Intanto che = 'while'/'meanwhile'; tu ti risense contains an archaic form risense (here reflexive ti + risense) meaning 'come back to yourself / recover (your) senses' (an older/perhaps dialectal perfective), followed by de la vista 'from the sight'—i.e. 'from the sight which...'. haï in me is the archaic 2nd-person singular present 'you have' (hai), and consunta is a past participle 'consumed/worn out' describing the sight.
segments.paradiso-26-g5: ben è che is an impersonal construction 'it is well/right that...' (ben = 'well'). ragionando is a gerund 'by reasoning / by speaking' indicating the manner in which the following should be done. la compense is a noun phrase; compense here means 'recompense/compensation/restitution' and the whole phrase states that it is proper that, by speaking, one repay or make up that which was taken.
segments.paradiso-26-g6: Comincia is a 2nd-person singular imperative 'begin' (command), and dunque 'then/therefore' is a discourse connective reinforcing the command.
segments.paradiso-26-g7: e dì is another imperative 'and say'. ove s’appunta is a subordinate clause: ove = 'where'; s'appunta is reflexive si + appunta (3rd person singular present) meaning 'is directed/points' (i.e. 'to what your soul turns'); l’anima tua is the noun phrase 'your soul'. The s’ is the reflexive clitic (si) attached to the verb (written with apostrophe).
segments.paradiso-26-g8: e fa ragion is an idiomatic instruction: fa (imperative or prescriptive 'make/do') + ragion ('account/reason') = 'give a reason'. che sia uses che + present subjunctive sia ('that it be') to introduce the content of the reason: la vista in te smarrita e non defunta = 'the sight in you lost/bewildered and not extinguished', where smarrita and defunta are past participles used adjectivally.
segments.paradiso-26-g9: perché = 'because'. la donna che per questa dia regïon ti conduce is a relative clause: che = 'who'; per + questa regïon = 'through/for this region' (per here = 'through'/'by way of'); dia is an archaic/subjunctive form of dare ('may give' or 'gives' in sense), used within the relative clause; conduce is present indicative 'leads/guides'.
segments.paradiso-26-g10: ha ne lo sguardo is literal 'has in her gaze' (ha = present 'has'; ne is the locative/partitive clitic 'in it/in her'); la virtù 'the power/virtue' is the subject of the relative clause ch’ebbe la man d’Anania, where ch' = che 'that' and ebbe is passato remoto 'had' (3rd sg) referring to the biblical Ananias; la man d’Anania = 'the hand (power/authority) of Ananias'.
segments.paradiso-26-g11: Io dissi uses the pronoun Io ('I') plus dissi, passato remoto (1st sg) of dire 'I said', a simple past narrative tense typical of Dante.
segments.paradiso-26-g12: Al suo piacere = 'at her pleasure' (Al = a + il). e tosto e tardo ('both soon and late') pairs two adverbs to cover either timing. vegna remedio uses vegna (archaic/poetic spelling of venga, present subjunctive of venire used optatively: 'may come') + remedio 'remedy' and a li occhi 'to the eyes' (a + article li = to the eyes).
segments.paradiso-26-g13: che introduces a relative clause modifying 'the eyes': fuor porte is an archaic phrase meaning 'outside the gates/doors' (fuor = fuori), while quand' ella entrò = 'when she entered' (entrò = passato remoto). col foco = 'with the fire' (con + il elided), ond' = archaic relative 'from which/whence', io sempr' ardo = 'I always burn' (sempr' = sempre elided; ardo = present 'I burn').
segments.paradiso-26-g14: Lo ben is a neuter/abstract noun phrase 'the good' (lo used as neuter article). che fa contenta questa corte is a relative clause: fa (3rd sg present 'makes') + contenta (adjective 'content') taking questa corte as its subject: 'the good that makes this court content.'
segments.paradiso-26-g15: Alfa e O is a poetic formula (Alpha and O, i.e. beginning and end). è di quanta scrittura uses è (present 'is') + di + quanta scrittura ('of all the writing/text'), where quanta is partitive/relative 'all that/every' and scrittura = 'writing/text'. mi legge Amore is 'Love reads to me' (legge = 3rd sg present 'reads', mi = to me), and o lievemente o forte ('either gently or strongly') lists the manners in which Love reads.
segments.paradiso-26-g16: "Quella medesma voce" = "that very same voice" (demonstrative + emphatic adjective + noun). The relative clause "che paura tolta m’avea del sùbito abbarbaglio" is built with the past participle "tolta" + the imperfect auxiliary "avea" (had taken away), with the object "paura" fronted; the whole means "which had removed from me the fear caused by the sudden glare." Word order is archaic/poetic but grammatical.
segments.paradiso-26-g17: "di ragionare ancor mi mise in cura" = literal order: "it put me into the care/concern of reasoning again." "mise" is the past preterite (mise) of mettere = "put"; "di ragionare" is the infinitive complement (to reason); "ancor" = "again/still" and "in cura" = "into care/concern" (here: to the task of thinking).
segments.paradiso-26-g18: "e disse:" = "and (it) said:" — "disse" is the passato remoto (simple past) of dire, used to report speech; "e" simply coordinates with the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-26-g19: "Certo a più angusto vaglio ti conviene schiarar:" — "Certo" = "certainly/indeed"; "a più angusto vaglio" = "in a more narrow scrutiny/test" (angusto = narrow; vaglio = scrutiny/examination); "ti conviene" is impersonal/dative construction meaning "it is fitting/necessary for you"; "schiarar" is the infinitive "schiarare" with the final -e dropped (poetic), meaning "to make clear/clarify."
segments.paradiso-26-g20: "dicer convienti chi drizzò l’arco tuo a tal berzaglio" — here "dicer" is the older infinitive form of "dire" (to say) and "convienti" (archaic/poetic form related to "conviene") expresses that it is fitting/expedient to say. The subordinate clause "chi drizzò l’arco tuo a tal berzaglio" = "who directed/aimed your bow at such a target"; "drizzò" is past tense (directed/aimed).
segments.paradiso-26-g21: "E io:" = a brief reporting formula: "And I:" — "E" coordinates and "io" is the first-person subject pronoun marking the speaker's reply.
segments.paradiso-26-g22: "Per filosofici argomenti e per autorità che quinci scende cotale amor convien che in me si ’mprenti:" — "Per" = "by/through"; "filosofici argomenti" = philosophical reasons; "autorità che quinci scende" = "authority that descends from here" (relative clause modifying "autorità"); "cotale amor convien che in me si ’mprenti" uses impersonal "convien" (it is necessary/fitting) + subordinate clause with the reflexive/passive "si ’mprenti" (archaic form of "imprenta/imprima") meaning "be imprinted/ impressed upon me." The infinitive/subjunctive morphology is archaic but expresses obligation/necessity.
segments.paradiso-26-g23: "ché ’l bene," = "for the good," where "ché" = "because/for" and "’l bene" = the noun "the good" (goodness) introduced to explain why the love must be imprinted.
segments.paradiso-26-g24: "in quanto ben," = "insofar as it is good"; "in quanto" is a concessive/limiting phrase, and "ben" here is the noun "good" (old spelling).
segments.paradiso-26-g25: "come s’intende," = "as is understood" — "come" = "as" and "s’intende" is the reflexive/impersonal present of "intendere" meaning "is understood/one understands."
segments.paradiso-26-g26: "così accende amore," = "thus kindles love" — "così" = thus/so, "accende" present of "accendere" = kindle/ignite, and "amore" = love (object of the verb).
segments.paradiso-26-g27: "e tanto maggio quanto più di bontate in sé comprende." — "tanto maggio" is an archaic comparative: "so much the greater" (maggio = maggiore). The construction "quanto più ... quanto ..." expresses proportion: "the greater, the more of goodness it contains in itself." "comprende" = present "contains/includes."
segments.paradiso-26-g28: "Dunque a l’essenza ov’ è tanto avvantaggio," — "Dunque" = therefore; "a l’essenza" = "to the Essence" (archaic spacing of a + article); "ov’" = "ove/ove è" = "where"; "tanto avvantaggio" = "so much advantage/benefit." This is the main directional phrase introducing the following reasoning.
segments.paradiso-26-g29: "che ciascun ben che fuor di lei si trova altro non è ch’un lume di suo raggio," — "che" introduces an explanatory clause: "that every good which is found outside it is nothing but a light of its ray." "fuor di lei" = "outside of it"; "si trova" is reflexive/impersonal present "is found"; "altro non è ch'" = "is nothing other than."
segments.paradiso-26-g30: "più che in altra convien che si mova la mente," — "più che in altra" = "more than elsewhere/than in another [place]"; "convien che si mova la mente" uses impersonal "convien" + subjunctive "si mova" (present subjunctive of "muovere") meaning "it is fitting/necessary that the mind be moved." The reflexive "si mova" gives a passive/impersonal shade: "the mind be moved."
segments.paradiso-26-g31: "amando," — gerund/participle of "amare" used adverbially: "in loving" or "while loving," qualifying how the mind is moved (i.e., moved by love).
segments.paradiso-26-g32: "di ciascun che cerne il vero in che si fonda questa prova." — "di" here has an agentive/partitive sense ("by/from each one"); "ciascun che cerne il vero" = "each one who discerns/perceives the truth" ("cerne" = present of "cernere" = "discern"); "in che si fonda questa prova" = "in which this proof is founded/grounded." Note: the supplied lexicon entry for "cerne" did not match the usual sense here (discern), so that item is marked as unknown below.
segments.paradiso-26-g33: "Tal vero" = "such truth"; the clause "a l’intelletto mïo sterne colui che mi dimostra..." has inverted word order: "sterne" (present of "sternere") is the verb meaning "spreads/places before" and agrees with the following subject "colui" (he who). So the literal order is "Such truth to my intellect spreads he who shows me...", i.e. "Such truth is presented to my intellect by the one who shows me..." The remainder names the thing shown: "the first love of all the everlasting substances."
segments.paradiso-26-g44: Non fu latente = 'was not latent/hidden.' Fu is the passato remoto (third singular) of essere and takes the adjective latente as a predicate agreeing with intenzione. Non negates the whole verb phrase. de l’aguglia di Cristo is a genitive phrase ('of the eagle of Christ'); de + l’ is an archaic/poetic form of di + l’.
segments.paradiso-26-g45: anzi = 'on the contrary/indeed' linking the two clauses. m’accorsi is mi (clitic) + accorsi, passato remoto 'I perceived/realized' (the clitic attaches to the verb). dove volea menar mia professione is a subordinate clause: dove = 'where,' volea = imperfect (3rd sing.) of volere in archaic spelling, 'intended/wished,' with an implicit subject ('he' = the eagle/Christ). menar = infinitive 'to lead'; mia professione = 'my vocation/profession/declaration.'
segments.paradiso-26-g46: Però ricominciai = 'Therefore/so I began again.' Ricominciai is passato remoto (1st sing.) of ricominciare, marking the start of the reported speech; the colon introduces the quotation.
segments.paradiso-26-g47: Tutti quei morsi = 'all those pangs.' Che posson far lo cor volgere a Dio is a relative clause modifying morsi: posson (archaic third-person plural of potere) + infinitive far + infinitive volgere = 'can make/bring the heart to turn to God.' Lo cor is the topicalized article + noun 'the heart.'
segments.paradiso-26-g48: a la mia caritate son concorsi = 'have converged into my charity.' The prepositional phrase a la mia caritate is fronted; son concorsi is a perfect/result state: son (sono) with the past participle concorsi (they have come together/are joined) agrees with the plural subject 'tutti quei morsi' understood from the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-26-g49: ché l’essere del mondo e l’esser mio = 'for the being/existence of the world and my being.' Ché = 'for/because.' L’essere and l’esser are nominalized infinitives (essere) used as nouns ('the being'). Del mondo = of the world; l’esser mio = my being/existence.
segments.paradiso-26-g50: la morte ch’el sostenne perch’ io viva = 'the death that he endured so that I may live.' Ch’el = che + el (archaic 'he'); sostenne is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of sostenere meaning 'suffered/endured.' Perch’ (= perché) here has a purposive sense 'so that,' and io viva uses viva as a present subjunctive expressing purpose/result ('that I may live').
segments.paradiso-26-g51: e quel che spera ogne fedel com’ io = 'and that which every faithful one hopes for as I do.' Quel che is a relative clause head 'that which'; spera is present indicative 'hopes'; ogne fedel = 'every faithful person'; com’ io = 'as I (do)' (com’ = come).
segments.paradiso-26-g52: con la predetta conoscenza viva = 'with the aforesaid living/vivid knowledge.' Con + definite article + predetta (aforesaid) modifies conoscenza (knowledge); viva is an adjective after the noun, here meaning 'living' or 'vivid' knowledge—an intensifying quality.
segments.paradiso-26-g53: tratto m’hanno del mar de l’amor torto = 'have drawn me from the sea of perverse/crooked love.' Tratto is the past participle used with hanno (the clitic m’ appears before hanno: m’hanno) to form the perfect passive/active sense 'they have drawn me.' del mar de l’amor torto = 'from the sea of twisted/wrong love' (metaphorical).
segments.paradiso-26-g54: e del diritto m’han posto a la riva = 'and by/rightness have placed me on the shore.' Del diritto ('of the right' = righteousness/right) names the source/means; m’han (mi + hanno) posto is the perfect 'have put/placed me'; a la riva = 'to the shore.' The clitic mi appears before hanno.
segments.paradiso-26-g55: Le fronde = 'the branches.' Onde s’infronda tutto l’orto = 'with which/whereby the whole garden is leafed/covered' (onde = relative 'wherewith' or 'from which'); s’infronda is a pronominal verb form (si + infronda) agreeing with orto (third singular): the garden is made leafy by the branches. de l’ortolano etterno = 'of the eternal gardener' (etterno = archaic spelling of eterno).
segments.paradiso-26-g56: am’ io cotanto quanto da lui a lor di bene è porto = 'I love (them) so much as the good that is brought to them by him.' am’ io = poetic contraction of amo io (I love); cotanto quanto = 'so much/as much as'; da lui (from him), a lor (to them), di bene (of good) è porto = 'is brought' (è + porto used in a passive sense here: 'is brought/carried to them'). Word order is poetic; the clause states the measure of the speaker's love by comparing it to the good that comes from him.
segments.paradiso-26-g57: Sì com’ io tacqui = 'just as/as soon as I fell silent.' Sì com’ = 'just as/so as'; io tacqui uses tacqui (passato remoto, 1st sing. of tacere) 'I became silent.'
segments.paradiso-26-g58: un dolcissimo canto risonò per lo cielo = 'a most sweet song resounded through the sky/heaven.' Dolcissimo is a superlative adjective modifying canto; risonò is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of risonare 'resounded'; per lo cielo = 'through the heaven/heavens.'
segments.paradiso-26-g59: e la mia donna dicea con li altri = 'and my lady was saying with the others.' Dicea is the imperfect (3rd sing.) 'was saying' (ongoing action); con li altri = 'with the others.'
segments.paradiso-26-g60: «Santo, = vocative/exclamatory 'Holy,' repeated as part of the liturgical cry. Santo is an adjective used as an address/exclamation.
segments.paradiso-26-g61: santo, = repetition of the exclamation 'holy,' for emphasis (liturgical triple acclamation).
segments.paradiso-26-g62: santo!» = final repetition of 'holy' concluding the triple refrain (common in liturgical texts: 'Holy, holy, holy').
segments.paradiso-26-g97: 'Or' is an adverbial discourse marker meaning 'now' or 'at this point' (archaic/poetic but common in Dante). It introduces the statement that follows and links back to the previous reasoning.
segments.paradiso-26-g98: 'figluol mio' is a vocative address: 'figluol' is an archaic spelling of 'figliol'/'figlio' ('son'), and 'mio' is the possessive following the noun (poetic word order). Together they mean 'my son', directly addressing the addressee.
segments.paradiso-26-g99: This clause contains a nominalized infinitive as subject: 'il gustar del legno' = 'the tasting/biting of the tree' (article + infinitive used as a noun). 'Non' negates the whole clause; 'fu' is the passato remoto (simple past) of 'essere' meaning 'was'; 'per sé' means 'in itself' or 'by itself'; the predicate is 'la cagion di tanto essilio' = 'the cause of so great an exile.' The word order (negation + subject + verb + predicate) is normal in Dante's prose/verse.
segments.paradiso-26-g100: 'Ma solamente' is a contrastive phrase: 'but only / rather only.' 'Il trapassar' is another nominalized infinitive ('the passing/exceeding'); note the archaic infinitive without a final -e ('trapassar' instead of modern 'trapassare'). 'Del segno' specifies what was passed — 'of the sign' (i.e., the prohibition or boundary). The clause gives the real causal element (the transgression) contrasted with the mere tasting.
segments.paradiso-26-g101: 'Quindi' here means 'from there' (or 'from that point'); 'onde' is a relative/adverbial 'whence/from which' linking to that place. 'Mosse' is the passato remoto of 'muovere' and here means 'set in motion' or 'set out' (i.e. caused to move). The phrase 'tua donna Virgilio' should be read as 'your lady (who) set Virgil [in motion]'—word order is compact/poetic.
segments.paradiso-26-g102: 'Quattromilia trecento e due volumi' is a numeral expression ('four thousand, three hundred and two volumes/books'). 'Di sol desiderai questo concilio' uses 'di sol' (short for 'di solo') to mean 'for/only' or 'solely' — here the poet says he longed for this counsel as much as those many volumes (a figurative way of expressing intense desire). 'Desiderai' is the first-person passato remoto 'I desired/wished for'.
segments.paradiso-26-g103: This coordinated clause: 'e vidi lui tornare' = 'and I saw him return.' 'Vidi' is passato remoto 'I saw'; 'lui' is emphatic/demonstrative 'him' as object. 'Tornare a tutt' i lumi de la sua strada' = 'return to all the lights of his road/path' — 'lumi' here are celestial or guiding lights (poetic). 'Novecento trenta fïate' gives the number of times ('nine hundred thirty times'); 'fïate' (from 'fiata') means 'times/occasions' in this context (Dante's usage).
segments.paradiso-26-g104: 'Mentre' = 'while'; 'ch’' (che) is the conjunction ('that/when') introducing the subordinate clause. 'ïo' = 'I'; 'in terra' = 'on earth'/'in the world'; 'fu’mi' is an archaic/poetic contracted past form: 'fu' (past of 'essere') with the enclitic 'mi', yielding 'I was' / 'it was to me' in the sense 'while I was on earth.' The whole clause states the temporal frame for the previous seeing.
segments.paradiso-26-g105: La lingua ch’io parlai = 'The language that I spoke.' ch’ is the elided che introducing a relative clause with subject io + parlai (passato remoto 'I spoke'). Fu tutta spenta is a stative/result clause using fu (past of essere) + the participle/adjective spenta 'extinguished/quenched' with tutta as an intensifier 'entirely.' Innanzi che governs the subordinate clause that follows and takes the imperfect subjunctive fosse ('were'/'might be') in classical/poetic syntax.
segments.paradiso-26-g106: Ché introduces an explanatory phrase ('for'/'because'). The phrase nullo effetto mai razïonabile is an emphatic negative: nullo modifies effetto ('no/none effect'), mai reinforces 'ever' (here functioning with the negation to mean 'ever' → 'never'), and razïonabile is an adjectival postmodifier 'reasonable.' Word order places the negative phrase up front for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-26-g107: Per lo piacere uman che rinovella = 'Through/by the human pleasure that renews itself.' Per expresses cause/instrumentality. Lo piacere uman is the noun phrase 'the human pleasure' with uman used adjectivally. Che introduces a relative clause whose verb rinovella is third-person singular present ('renews'/'is renewed'). Seguendo il cielo is a gerundial phrase 'following the heavens' (i.e., according to the celestial motions) describing the manner by which this pleasure renews itself.
segments.paradiso-26-g108: Sempre fu durabile = 'was ever/always durable.' Fu is the past tense of essere ('was') and durabile is a predicative adjective 'lasting/durable.' Note the scope of negation: the earlier nullo ... mai makes this clause read negatively ('never was durable'); sempre here emphasizes temporal extent and is used with the prior negative to mean 'at any time/ever.'
segments.paradiso-26-g109: Opera naturale è ch’uom favella = a classic impersonal formulation meaning 'It is natural (a natural work) that man speaks.' Opera naturale functions as a predicative noun phrase and è links it to a subordinate clause introduced by che. Uom is the subject ('man') and favella is an archaic present-form verb meaning 'speaks' (third-person singular); favella here is not a noun but the verb 'to speak' in older diction.
segments.paradiso-26-g110: Ma così o così is an idiomatic parenthesis: ma = 'but,' così o così = 'this way or that' / 'one way or another.' It signals a shift or qualification of the general statement just made.
segments.paradiso-26-g111: Natura lascia poi fare a voi secondo che v’abbella = 'Nature then leaves (allows) you to act according to what pleases you.' Lascia + fare is the idiom 'lets/makes possible to do.' A voi marks the indirect object 'to you.' Secondo che introduces the subordinate clause; v’ is the clitic vi 'to/for you' attached to the verb abbella (third-person present), which here means 'pleases you' or 'suits you'—so v’abbella = 'it pleases you' or 'as you like.'
segments.paradiso-26-g63: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period). In the poem it marks a full stop/pause between lines; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.paradiso-26-g64: 'E come a lume acuto si disonna...' is a simile head: 'E come' = 'And as'. 'a lume acuto' places 'a' + noun phrase meaning 'at/by a sharp light' (preposition 'a' used locatively). 'si' is the reflexive/impersonal marker making 'disonna' an intransitive/impersonal form ('one is discomposed' / 'is thrown out of tune'). 'disonna' is an archaic/poetic verb (from disonnare) meaning to be made discordant/dazed; the clause continues with a relative clause introduced by 'che' that specifies what causes the effect.
segments.paradiso-26-g65: This short clause continues the simile: 'e lo svegliato ciò che vede aborre,'—'e' = 'and'. 'lo svegliato' is an adjectival/past participle phrase 'the awakened [part]' (svegliato) referring to the awakened faculty; 'ciò che vede' is a relative clause 'that which it sees'; 'aborre' is present subjunctive/indicative-archaic form meaning 'abhors/loathes' and takes 'ciò che vede' as its object.
segments.paradiso-26-g66: 'sì nescïa è la sùbita vigilia fin che la stimativa non soccorre;' — 'sì' = 'so/thus'; 'nescïa' (nescia) is an adjective 'ignorant/unaware'; copula 'è' links it to 'la sùbita vigilia' ('the sudden wakefulness'). 'fin che' = 'until' introduces the subordinate clause; 'la stimativa' names the estimating/assessing faculty, and 'non soccorre' is present tense 'does not come to help' (soccorre = 'comes to aid'), literally 'until the estimative does not come to aid' → idiomatically 'until the estimating faculty comes to its help'.
segments.paradiso-26-g67: 'così de li occhi miei ogne quisquilia fugò Beatrice col raggio d’i suoi,' — 'così' = 'thus/so' linking back to the simile; 'de li occhi miei' = 'from my eyes' (archaic 'de' for 'da/di' + article 'li'); 'ogne quisquilia' = 'every trifle/rubbish' (direct object). 'fugò' is perfect/past 'drove away/dispelled' with Beatrice as subject; 'col raggio d'i suoi' = 'with the ray of hers' ('col' = 'con il', 'd'i' = 'di i').
segments.paradiso-26-g68: 'che rifulgea da più di mille milia:' — 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying 'raggio' ('which'); 'rifulgea' is an imperfect/poetic form meaning 'shone/resplended'; 'da più di mille milia' literally 'from more than a thousand thousands' (a poetic hyperbole meaning an immense number), where 'più di' = 'more than'.
segments.paradiso-26-g69: 'onde mei che dinanzi vidi poi;' — 'onde' here = 'whence/wherefore/therefore' (poetic connective pointing back to the previous brightness), 'mei' = 'of me/my' (poetic order), 'che' is a relative/conjunctive particle, 'dinanzi vidi poi' = 'I saw before, afterwards' (literal order is inverted: 'what I had previously seen I then saw again/afterwards').
segments.paradiso-26-g70: 'e quasi stupefatto domandai d’un quarto lume ch’io vidi tra noi.' — 'e' = 'and'; 'quasi stupefatto' = 'almost stupefied/astonished' (quasi + past participle used adverbially); 'domandai' = simple past 'I asked' (first person); 'd’un quarto lume' = 'about a fourth light' (the object of the question); 'ch’io vidi tra noi' = relative clause 'that I saw among us', with 'ch’' = 'che' introducing it and 'vidi' simple past 'I saw'.
segments.paradiso-26-g71: 'E la mia donna:' is a nominative phrase introducing direct speech: 'And my lady:' (the colon introduces Beatrice's spoken words). 'la mia donna' is a respectful/title phrase—here 'donna' means Dante's beloved/guide Beatrice rather than a generic woman.
segments.paradiso-26-g72: This is the quoted sentence. 'Dentro da quei rai' = 'within/from those rays' (prepositional phrase setting location). 'vagheggia' is present indicative 'gazes upon/longs for/adoringly contemplates' and takes 'il suo fattor' ('its Maker') as direct object; word order is inverted: verb + object + subject 'l’anima prima' ('the first soul') follows. 'che la prima virtù creasse mai' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'che' after 'prima' meaning 'before the first virtue ever created' — 'creasse' is imperfect-subjunctive (poetic subjunctive) used after 'prima (che)' to mean 'before ... ever [did].' 'mai' reinforces 'ever'.
segments.paradiso-26-g73: 'Come la fronda che flette la cima nel transito del vento,' is a simile beginning: 'Come' = 'As/like'. 'la fronda' = 'the leafy bough/branch'; 'che flette la cima' is a relative clause 'that bends the top/tip' ('flette' = 'bends'); 'nel transito del vento' = 'in the passing of the wind' where 'nel' = 'in the' and 'transito' = 'passing/passage', so the whole phrase pictures a branch bent by a gust.
segments.paradiso-26-g74: 'e poi si leva per la propria virtù che la soblima,' continues the simile: 'and then it rises up by its own virtue/power that sublimates/exalts it.' 'si leva' is reflexive 'rises' (the branch rises again on its own), 'per la propria virtù' = 'by its own power/faculty'. 'che la soblima' is a relative clause 'which elevates/sublimates it' (here 'soblima' is a poetic/archaic form meaning to exalt or lift up).
segments.paradiso-26-g75: 'fec’ io in tanto in quant’ ella diceva,' — 'fec' (poetic contraction of 'feci') + 'io' = 'I made/did' (here 'I was doing' / 'I acted'). 'in tanto' = 'in the meantime/as long as', 'in quant'' = 'inasmuch as/while', so the full phrase means 'I did [this] meanwhile while she was speaking' with 'ella diceva' the imperfect 'she was saying/speaking'.
segments.paradiso-26-g76: 'stupendo,' is a single-word exclamation/adjectival past participle meaning 'astonished' or 'being astonished'; it describes the speaker's state during the previous clause (elliptical 'I was astonished').
segments.paradiso-26-g77: 'e poi mi rifece sicuro un disio di parlare ond’ ïo ardeva.' — 'e poi' = 'and then'; 'mi rifece' = 'restored to me' (rifecere: made/returned), 'sicuro' = 'secure/confident' (so 'restored my confidence'); 'un disio di parlare' = 'a desire to speak' (object of rifecce); 'ond’ ïo ardeva' = 'wherewith/with which I was burning' (the relative/adverb 'ond'' = 'from which/whereby' and 'ardeva' = 'was burning' meaning burning with desire). Together: the desire to speak that I burned with restored my confidence.
segments.paradiso-26-g112: Pria ch’ (= 'prima che') introduces a temporal clause and requires the subjunctive: scendessi is the imperfect subjunctive of scendere ('that I should descend' / 'before I descended'). The clitic i (here written i’) is the subject pronoun 'I' (io) attached in old orthography; a + l’ = a + il (to the), and infernale ambascia is a noun phrase (adjective after noun in poetic order).
segments.paradiso-26-g113: s'appellava is the pronominal form of appellare used impersonally/passively ('was called' / 'used to be called'); word order places the topic-form 'I' before the verb for emphasis. onde is an adverb ('whence') linking to the following clause; vien is present indicative (comes), and mi fascia is present indicative where mi is the first‑person object and fascia (from fasciare) means 'enfolds/envelops' here.
segments.paradiso-26-g114: El is the archaic subject pronoun ('he'); si chiamò is passato remoto (chiamare) with the reflexive clitic, here functioning as 'was called' or 'called himself' in narrative past; poi = 'then/afterwards'. The conjunction e simply links this clause to the previous one.
segments.paradiso-26-g115: e ciò convene is a short declarative phrase: ciò ('this/that') is a demonstrative pronoun, and convene is the present indicative (3rd sing.) of convenire meaning 'is fitting' or 'is right/appropriate' in literary register.
segments.paradiso-26-g116: ché (= perché) introduces a causal clause 'because'; l’uso d’i mortali is a noun phrase with the elided article l’ and the contracted preposition d’ + i ('of the mortals' = of human beings). è is the copula 'is' and come introduces a simile; fronda (feminine) and in ramo form the expression 'like foliage on a branch'.
segments.paradiso-26-g117: che is the relative pronoun 'which/that'; sen va is the contracted se n(= se ne) + va meaning 'goes away' or 'falls off' (the reflexive/partitive particle ne combined with se), e altra vene means 'and another comes' — vene is a poetic/older form of viene ('comes') and altra agrees with fronda (feminine).
segments.paradiso-26-g118: Nel monte = 'on/in the mountain' (in + il). che si leva is a relative clause: si leva is the pronominal verb (riflessive/passive) meaning 'rises up'; più da l’onda means 'higher/from the wave' (più = more / higher, da l’ = da + il = from the).
segments.paradiso-26-g119: fu’ is the passato remoto (fu = fu) of essere ('was'); the apostrophe in fu’ is orthographic. The following io is the subject pronoun 'I' and is emphatic after the verb: 'I was'.
segments.paradiso-26-g120: con vita pura e disonesta is an ablative/associative phrase 'with a life pure and disonest/indecent'; vita is feminine so the adjectives pura and disonesta agree in gender and number. The pairing pura e disonesta is intentionally paradoxical (a common poetic device).
segments.paradiso-26-g121: da la prim’ ora a quella che seconda is a temporal span: da ... a = 'from ... to'; prim’ (prima) ora = 'the first hour'; quella che seconda = 'the one which is second' (i.e. the following hour). The relative che introduces 'which follows'.
segments.paradiso-26-g122: come introduces a comparison 'as/when'; ’l sol is elided il sol ('the sun') and muta is present indicative of mutare here meaning 'changes' (moves/changing position), while quadra is an archaic noun meaning 'quarter' or 'section' of the sky — together 'as the sun changes quarter'. These are poetic/archaic forms.
segments.paradiso-26-g123: l’ora sesta = 'the sixth hour' (l’ = the elided article; ora feminine noun; sesta = ordinal adjective agreeing with ora). The final punctuation marks the close of the quoted speech.
segments.paradiso-26-g78: "E" is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this speech to the preceding action; "cominciai" is the first-person singular passato remoto of cominciare, meaning 'I began' (here: 'I began to speak').
segments.paradiso-26-g79: "O" is the vocative interjection 'O/Oh'; "pomo" is a vocative noun 'fruit'; the relative clause "che maturo solo prodotto fosti" modifies pomo: "che" = 'that/which'; "maturo" is used adjectivally/past-participial to mean 'ripe/mature'; "solo" = 'only'; "prodotto" is the past participle 'produced'; "fosti" is the passato remoto of essere ('you were') used poetically with the vocative noun (sense of 'you were produced/you came to be').
segments.paradiso-26-g80: "o padre antico" is a second vocative: 'O ancient father'. "a cui" is a dative relative ('to whom'); it introduces the clause "cui ciascuna sposa è figlia e nuro" where "ciascuna sposa" = 'each bride/wife' is subject and "è" = present indicative 'is'; "figlia e nuro" = 'daughter and daughter-in-law' (nuro = archaic form for 'daughter-in-law').
segments.paradiso-26-g81: "divoto quanto posso" = 'devoted/as pious as I can be' (divoto = 'devout/devoted', quanto posso = 'as much as I can'); "a te supplìco" = 'I beseech you' where "supplìco" is first-person present (I beg) and "a te" marks the addressee; "perché mi parli" expresses purpose ('so that you speak to me') and here "parli" is present subjunctive, after perche used purposively ('so that').
segments.paradiso-26-g82: "tu vedi mia voglia" is a simple present statement with an emphatic subject pronoun: "tu" + present "vedi" = 'you see'; "mia voglia" = 'my desire/wish' (direct object). Word order emphasizes the addressee's awareness.
segments.paradiso-26-g83: "e per udirti tosto non la dico" literally 'and in order to hear you immediately I do not say it'. "per udirti" uses the infinitive with the enclitic object "-ti" ('to hear you' / 'so as to hear you'); "tosto" = 'quickly/immediately'; the negation "non" with "la" (feminine pronoun referring to the desire) precedes the verb "dico" ('I say'), so the speaker withholds the expressed desire in order to listen first.
segments.paradiso-26-g84: "Talvolta" is an adverb 'sometimes'; "un animal coverto" = 'a covered/hidden animal' (adjective coverto following the noun); "broglia" here functions as a verb/noun meaning 'plots/contrives' or 'deceives' (archaic usage), so the whole phrase says that the concealed creature schemed or hid its intent.
segments.paradiso-26-g85: "sì che" = 'so that/therefore'; "l'affetto" is the subject 'the feeling/affection'; "convien" is an impersonal present ('it is necessary/it must'); after "convien" the clause takes a subordinate verb, here "che si paia" where "si" is impersonal/reflexive and "paia" (from parere) means 'appear' or 'be shown' (subjunctive/indicative nuance governed by convien). "per lo seguir che face a lui la 'nvoglia" = 'according to the following/sequence that the desire makes toward it' — "seguir" and "nvoglia" are archaic nouns/forms and "face" is an older form of 'fa' ('makes/does').
segments.paradiso-26-g86: "e similmente" = 'and similarly'; "l'anima primaia" = 'the first soul' (primaia an archaic adjective 'first/earliest'); "mi facea trasparer" uses the imperfect "facea" ('was making/causing') + infinitive "trasparer" ('to show through' / 'to appear through'), meaning 'caused me to perceive through the veil/covering'; "per la coverta" = 'through the covering'; "quant' ella a compiacermi venìa gaia" = 'how much she came, joyful, to please me' — "venìa" is imperfect 'was coming/used to come' and "compiacermi" has the enclitic object "-mi" ('to please me').
segments.paradiso-26-g87: "Indi" is an adverb meaning 'then/thence'; "spirò" is the passato remoto of spirare and here means 'she breathed/exhaled' (i.e., she uttered or breathed out the words), a poetic, concise way to introduce speech.
segments.paradiso-26-g88: "Sanz'" is a contraction of 'senza' meaning 'without'; "essermi proferta" is a passive/periphrastic construction: "essermi" = 'being to me / having been to me' (essere + clitic mi) and "proferta" is a past participle 'offered/presented', so the phrase means 'without it having been offered to me by you' — "da te" marks the agent 'by you'.
segments.paradiso-26-g89: "la voglia tua discerno meglio" = 'I discern your desire better' where "discerno" is present indicative 'I discern'; "meglio" = 'better'; the following comparative clause uses "che" in the sense 'than' (comparative), so: 'better than you (discern)...'; "t'è più certa" = contraction of 'ti è' meaning 'is to you more certain' (i.e. 'more certain to you').
segments.paradiso-26-g90: "perch' io la veggio" = 'because I see it' ("perch'" = 'because/for', "veggio" = present 'I see'); "nel verace speglio" = 'in the true mirror' (speglio = archaic for 'mirror/reflection'); the relative clause "che fa di sé pareglio a l’altre cose" = 'which makes itself like other things' where "fa" = present 'makes' and "di sé pareglio" = 'a likeness of itself'.
segments.paradiso-26-g91: "e nulla face lui di sé pareglio" is an emphatic/clipped clause: "e" = 'and', "nulla" = 'nothing', "face" (archaic form of 'fa') = 'makes/does', "lui" = emphatic pronoun 'it/he', and "di sé pareglio" = 'a likeness of itself'; word order places object before verb for emphasis: 'and it makes nothing that is like itself.'
segments.paradiso-26-g92: "Tu vuogli udir" is present second-person 'you want to hear'; "quant' è che Dio mi puose" is the idiom asking 'how long it is since God placed me' ("quant'è che" = 'how long it is since'); "puose" is passato remoto of porre ('placed/put'), and "mi" is the clitic indicating the speaker as recipient ('placed me'). "ne l'eccelso giardino" = 'in the exalted garden' (ne = in).
segments.paradiso-26-g93: "ove" = 'where'; "costei" here functions as an archaic demonstrative/pronoun meaning 'she/this woman' (the wording in the supplied sense list for costei is not applicable), so it introduces the clause 'where she...'; "a così lunga scala ti dispuose" = 'set/placed you upon such a long ladder/scale' — "dispuose" is passato remoto of disporre ('arranged/placed') and "ti" is the clitic object 'you'.
segments.paradiso-26-g94: "e quanto fu diletto a li occhi miei" = 'and how much delight there was to my eyes' — "quanto" = 'how much', "fu" passato remoto of essere 'was', "diletto" = 'delight/pleasure', and "a li occhi miei" = 'to my eyes' (indirect object expressing perception).
segments.paradiso-26-g95: "e la propria cagion del gran disdegno" = 'and the very cause of the great disdain' — "propria" emphasizes 'proper/own/true', "cagion" = 'cause/reason', and "del gran disdegno" = 'of the great disdain'.
segments.paradiso-26-g96: "e l'idïoma ch'usai e che fei" = 'and the idiom/language that I used and what I did' — "idïoma" = 'language/idiom', "usai" passato remoto 'I used', and "fei" passato remoto of fare 'I did/made'; the two coordinated clauses list the language employed and the actions performed.

sentences.paradiso-26-s9: The voice of the true Author spread forth, who says to Moses, speaking of himself: "I will make you see every power."
sentences.paradiso-26-s10: Again you spread it to me, beginning the lofty proclamation in which the secret cries out from here and there and below above every other proclamation.
sentences.paradiso-26-s11: And I heard: "By human intelligence and by authorities concordant with it, look to God regarding your loves, O sovereign."
sentences.paradiso-26-s12: But say furthermore whether you feel other strings drawing you toward him, so that you may sound forth with how many teeth this love bites you.
sentences.paradiso-26-s1: While I hesitated at the dimmed visage, from the resplendent flame that had extinguished it came forth a breath that made me attentive, saying: "Meanwhile, until you come back to yourself from the sight you have spent on me, it is right that, by speaking, you make amends for it."
sentences.paradiso-26-s2: Begin then; and say where your soul is directed, and give a reason that the sight in you is bewildered and not extinguished: for the lady who guides you through this region has in her gaze the power that Ananias' hand once had.
sentences.paradiso-26-s3: I said: 'At her pleasure, be it soon or late, let remedy come to the eyes that went outside the gates when she entered with the fire from which I always burn.'
sentences.paradiso-26-s4: The good that makes this court content is the Alpha and O of all the writing that Love reads to me, whether gently or strongly.
sentences.paradiso-26-s5: That same voice, which had taken from me the fear of the sudden dazzle, set me again to reasoning; and it said: "Surely you must be made clear by a closer scrutiny: it is fitting to say who directed your bow at such a mark."
sentences.paradiso-26-s6: And I: "By philosophical arguments and by the authority that descends from here, such love must be impressed upon me: for the good, insofar as it is good, as is understood, thus kindles love; and the greater it is, the more of goodness it contains in itself."
sentences.paradiso-26-s7: Therefore, to the Essence where there is so great an advantage — since every good found outside it is nothing but a light of its ray — the mind must be moved, in loving, more than elsewhere by each one who discerns the truth on which this proof is founded.
sentences.paradiso-26-s8: Such truth is spread before my intellect by him who shows me the first love of all the everlasting substances.
sentences.paradiso-26-s13: The holy intention of Christ’s eagle was not hidden; on the contrary, I perceived where he meant to lead my vocation.
sentences.paradiso-26-s14: So I began again: 'All those pangs that can make the heart turn to God have gathered into my charity: for the being of the world and my being, the death that he endured so that I may live, and what every faithful one hopes for as I do, with the aforesaid living knowledge, have drawn me from the sea of perverse love, and have set me on the shore of right.'
sentences.paradiso-26-s15: The branches with which the whole garden of the eternal gardener is leafy—I love them as much as the good that is brought to them from him.
sentences.paradiso-26-s16: As soon as I fell silent, a most sweet song resounded through the heavens, and my lady was saying with the others: 'Holy, holy, holy!'
sentences.paradiso-26-s25: Now, my son, it was not the tasting of the tree that, by itself, was the cause of so great an exile, but only the transgression of the sign.
sentences.paradiso-26-s26: From the place where your lady set Virgil in motion, I longed for this counsel in four thousand, three hundred and two volumes alone; and I saw him return to all the lights of his path nine hundred and thirty times while I was on earth.
sentences.paradiso-26-s27: The language I spoke was entirely extinguished before Nimrod’s people paid attention to the unconsummable work; for no reasonable effect ever, through the human pleasure that renews itself following the heavens, was lasting.
sentences.paradiso-26-s28: It is a natural act that man speaks; but one way or another, nature then lets you act as pleases you.
sentences.paradiso-26-s18: And as, at a sharp light, the visual spirit that turns toward the radiance which goes from gown to gown is thrown out of tune; and the awakened part loathes what it sees; so ignorant is sudden wakefulness until the estimating faculty comes to its aid; thus Beatrice drove every trifle from my eyes with the ray of hers, which shone from more than a thousand thousands; whence I afterward saw what I had seen before; and, almost stupefied, I asked about a fourth light that I saw among us.
sentences.paradiso-26-s19: And my lady: 'Within those rays the first soul gazes upon its Maker before the first virtue ever created [him].'
sentences.paradiso-26-s20: Like a leafy bough that bends its tip in the passing wind, and then rises up by its own power that exalts it, I did in the meantime, while she spoke, (be) astonished, and then a desire to speak, which I was burning with, made me confident again.
sentences.paradiso-26-s29: Before I descended into infernal anguish, on earth the supreme good was called "I," whence comes the joy that enfolds me; and He was afterwards called so, and that was fitting, because the custom of mortals is like foliage on a branch, which goes away and another comes.
sentences.paradiso-26-s30: On the mountain that rises highest from the wave I was, with a life pure and indecent, from the first hour to the one that follows, as the sun changes quarter, the sixth hour.
sentences.paradiso-26-s21: And I began: "O fruit that alone were produced ripe, O ancient father to whom every wife is both daughter and daughter-in-law, as devoted as I can I beg you to speak to me; you see my wish, and in order to hear you immediately I do not say it."
sentences.paradiso-26-s22: Sometimes a hidden animal plots, so that the feeling must appear according to the course that desire makes toward it; and similarly the first soul caused me to shine through the covering how, rejoicing, she came to please me.
sentences.paradiso-26-s23: Then she breathed: "Without your having offered it to me, I discern your desire better than you discern any thing that is most certain to you; for I see it in the true mirror which makes itself like other things, and it makes nothing of itself like itself."
sentences.paradiso-26-s24: You wish to hear how long it is since God placed me in the exalted garden, where she arranged you on so long a ladder, and how much delight there was to my eyes, and the very cause of the great disdain, and the idiom I used and what I did.
